perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the law of the United States of America and the State of New Jersey without regard to conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the law of the United States of America without regard to conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the reference to New Jersey state law could deprive the consumer of protections afforded specifically by New Jersey law, such as specific consumer protection statutes or regulations that might be more favorable to the consumer than federal law alone.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey Consumer Fraud Act, N.J.S.A. 56:8-1 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: The New Jersey Consumer Fraud Act prohibits unconscionable commercial practices, deception, fraud, false pretense, false promise, misrepresentation, or knowing concealment in connection with the sale of merchandise or real estate. Removing New Jersey law from the governing law clause potentially shields the company from liability under this act, which provides specific remedies to consumers.

The act, use or employment by any person of any commercial practice that is unconscionable or abusive, deception, fraud, false pretense, false promise, misrepresentation, or the knowing, concealment, suppression, or omission of any material fact with intent that others rely upon such concealment, suppression or omission, in connection with the sale or advertisement of any merchandise or real estate, or with the subsequent performance of such person as aforesaid, whether or not any person has in fact been misled, deceived or damaged thereby, is declared to be an unlawful practice; provided, however, that nothing herein contained shall apply to the owner or publisher of newspapers, magazines, publications or printed matter wherein such advertisement appears, or to the owner or operator of a radio or television station which disseminates such advertisement when the owner, publisher, or operator has no knowledge of the intent, design or purpose of the advertiser. L.1960, c. 39, p. 138, s. 2. Amended by L.1967, c. 301, s. 2, eff. Feb. 15, 1968; L.1971, c. 247, s.
perturbation.justification: The omission of New Jersey state law from the governing law clause directly contradicts the potential applicability of the New Jersey Consumer Fraud Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, NJ Rev Stat § 56:8-2 (2022), explicitly defines certain commercial practices as unlawful, offering consumer protection within New Jersey. By removing New Jersey law, the changed text seeks to avoid these protections, creating a legal contradiction by undermining consumer rights granted under New Jersey law. This creates a loophole for the company to potentially evade the provisions of the New Jersey Consumer Fraud Act.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall hold the Information in confidence, and shall use reasonable efforts to prevent any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall hold the Information in confidence.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'reasonable efforts' clause, the agreement now provides less protection against unauthorized disclosure. This could be problematic under state laws that require specific and demonstrable measures to protect consumer data. New Jersey law requires businesses to implement reasonable security measures to protect personal information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey Identity Theft Prevention Act, N.J.S.A. 56:15-1 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Identity Theft Prevention Act requires businesses to protect personal information and take reasonable measures to prevent unauthorized access. Removing the requirement for 'reasonable efforts' weakens the protection afforded to confidential information and may violate the spirit, if not the exact letter, of this law regarding consumer data protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: New Jersey Revised Statutes Section 56:15-4 (2022) - Entitlement to recovery of damages; exceptions. :: 2022 New Jersey Revised Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 N.J. Rev. Stat. 2023 N.J. Rev. Stat. 2022 N.J. Rev. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New Jersey Revised Statutes Title 56 - Trade Names, Trade-Marks and Unfair Trade Practices Section 56:15-4 - Entitlement to recovery of damages; exceptions. Universal Citation: NJ Rev Stat § 56:15-4 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 56:15-4 Entitlement to recovery of damages; exceptions. 4. a. Except to the extent that circumstances, including a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. b. If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award punitive damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection a. of this section. L.2011, c.161, s.4. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 'reasonable efforts' clause, which weakens the protection of confidential information. The law explanation suggests this may violate the Identity Theft Prevention Act's requirement for reasonable measures to prevent unauthorized access. Snippet 1, from New Jersey Revised Statutes Section 56:15-4, discusses damages for misappropriation. While it doesn't explicitly define 'reasonable efforts,' the omission of this requirement in the modified text potentially reduces the measures taken to prevent misappropriation, increasing the risk of damages and contradicting the spirit of consumer data protection laws.

perturbation.original_text: At Disclosing Party's request, the Recipient shall promptly return or destroy all Information disclosed by Disclosing Party and shall retain no copies.
perturbation.changed_text: At Disclosing Party's request, the Recipient shall return all Information disclosed by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'or destroy' and 'and shall retain no copies', the modified text now allows the Recipient to retain copies of confidential information even after the Disclosing Party has requested its return. This can conflict with data privacy laws that require companies to properly dispose of consumer data when requested, particularly in the context of data breaches or security incidents. While the agreement concerns business information, the principle of data minimization is broadly recognized in privacy regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey Notification of Data Security Breach Law, N.J.S.A. 56:8-163
perturbation.law_explanation: This New Jersey law mandates businesses to notify consumers if their personal information is compromised in a data breach. While not directly about destruction of data, retaining unauthorized copies increases the risk of a breach. If the retained information pertains to individuals (even indirectly through business contacts), the failure to destroy copies could exacerbate the impact of a data breach, potentially leading to violations if those individuals are not properly notified. It also skirts the general data minimization principle implied in such laws.

Universal Citation: NJ Rev Stat § 56:8-163 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 56:8-163 Disclosure of breach of security to customers. 12. a. Any business that conducts business in New Jersey, or any public entity that compiles or maintains computerized records that include personal information, shall disclose any breach of security of those computerized records following discovery or notification of the breach to any customer who is a resident of New Jersey whose personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, accessed by an unauthorized person. The disclosure to a customer shall be made in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay, consistent with the legitimate needs of law enforcement, as provided in subsection c. of this section, or any measures necessary to determine the scope of the breach and restore the reasonable integrity of the data system. Disclosure of a breach of security to a customer shall not be required under this section if the business or public entity establishes that misuse of the information is not reasonably possible. Any determination shall be documented in writing and retained for five years. b. Any business or public entity that compiles or maintains computerized records that include personal information on behalf of another business or public entity shall notify that business or public entity, who s
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the requirement for the Recipient to 'destroy' confidential information and to 'retain no copies'. This directly contradicts the principle of data minimization and increases the risk of data breaches, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While the provided law snippet (New Jersey Revised Statutes Section 56:8-163) focuses on breach notification, the Law Explanation correctly points out that retaining unauthorized copies exacerbates the risk of a breach and potential violations. The omission weakens data protection and compliance, because it allows the recipient to hold data indefinitely, increasing the likelihood of a data breach requiring notification under the statute.

perturbation.original_text: The parties hereby acknowledge and agree that in the event of any violation or a threatened violation of this Agreement by the Recipient, the Disclosing Party shall be authorized and entitled to seek from any court of competent jurisdiction preliminary and permanent injunctive relief.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties hereby acknowledge and agree that in the event of any violation of this Agreement by the Recipient, the Disclosing Party shall be authorized and entitled to seek injunctive relief.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'or a threatened violation' and 'preliminary and permanent,' the clause now only allows for injunctive relief after a violation has already occurred, and the type of injunction is left undefined which significantly limits the Disclosing Party's ability to prevent potential harm. New Jersey courts, like courts in many jurisdictions, have the power to grant preliminary injunctions to prevent irreparable harm before a full trial. This change could conflict with the Disclosing Party's rights under New Jersey law to seek preliminary relief to prevent data breaches or misuse of information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey Court Rule 4:52-1 - Injunctions During Pendency of Action
perturbation.law_explanation: This court rule outlines the process for obtaining preliminary injunctions in New Jersey. By limiting the injunctive relief to instances where a violation has already occurred and removing reference to preliminary relief, the contract effectively restricts the Disclosing Party's ability to seek immediate court intervention to prevent ongoing or imminent harm, potentially conflicting with their rights under New Jersey law and jurisprudence regarding equitable remedies.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the possibility of seeking injunctive relief for a 'threatened violation' and removes the specification that the injunctive relief can be 'preliminary'. The Law Explanation highlights that this conflicts with rights under New Jersey law regarding equitable remedies by restricting the Disclosing Party's ability to seek immediate court intervention to prevent ongoing or imminent harm. While there are no scraped law snippets provided, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation provide sufficient evidence to suggest the removal of the ability to seek preliminary injunctions creates a legal loophole, by removing a legally recognized form of equitable relief.
